Hey, this is Sean and Andre. It is the morning of October 7th, 2021. Today we're going to be discussing the Art of Email Fall 2021 enrollment. This is for the live version of the workshop that we're doing the first time through. And Andre is going to start us off with an overview. And then we're just going to talk through the various components of the enrollment, things that we think are interesting. And then we'll take your questions next week in the first Q&A call. So Andre, you want to start with an overview so people understand what they're seeing here on the screen? Yeah, so this is our amazing visual. And so the guy who created the visual. So this line over here is our value newsletter. This line over here represents the bridge emails that we sent out. So on September 10th was this email here, this newsletter there, and this newsletter on the 24th, we decided beforehand that there were going to be around the theme of email, seeing we were promoting an email course. And so this is part one and this one was part two of that, which is available on our site now. And this one, there was no way where people could segment themselves into this promotion. This was just us demonstrating value, giving something where they didn't need to do anything. But we did mention that watch out for next week when we get to give people the opportunity to raise the hand if they're interested in this, which is kind of what these weird squiggles are. So that was just queuing this stuff over here up. And we then sent out that newsletter. So then at that point, everybody over here now knows a few things. They know that the following week, there's going to be some mention or ability for them to raise their hand if they're interested in this thing. And they also know that part two of that newsletter that we're going to get on the 24th. So there was kind of two things for them to look forward to. And I think it's important to interject here too, just to clarify the image that say it right up front, you're going to see two different tracks. So when you get into words as solo and the hard bridge, there are two tracks because there are actually two audiences. One, there's an audience of people who already own the art of email, and they're getting their notification tells them they're getting the art of email for free. They're they're I'm sorry, they owned autoresponder madness, they were getting the upgrade to the art of email for free. And then there is a parallel track of people who don't didn't own the art of email or didn't own how to respond to madness. So it's different messaging. And you'll see, you can see in the diagram that it goes there to two tracks that run side by side. And that's what those two distinctions are. And one of the reasons we one of the reasons we couldn't actually segment from the September 10 value newsletter, we didn't ever consider doing that. But we couldn't anyway, because the bridge emails needed to go based, they needed to be sent based on whether or not somebody already owned autoresponder madness. So we had to do the bridge emails this way. So that's just I think an important consideration to mention. Yeah, so it was easy because obviously we have we have segments for our for our customers, or respond to madness, got the email if she wants to say. And then the other bridge email that was sent out to everybody. So the other one went out to everybody minus customers of autoresponder madness. So that's that's how we how we created those, those two segments. And then obviously people needed to raise their hand. And this this image just kind of demonstrates the the relative size of those segments. Obviously, this, this hard bridge went out to our entire audience minus the customs, which is why it's the biggest segment. And then we did that twice. We talked about that too, I think it's important to just draw everyone's attention to we had this conversation beforehand, should we run one campaign? We could have two different audiences, but it's really the same enrollment. But the challenge with that would, you know, if we want to speak to each person as an individual and their individual needs, we realized very quickly that if somebody already owned autoresponder madness, and they were going, they, they, they got the art of email, and we wanted to talk with them about the live enrollment, the live workshop, that that's different. Like we could have made one campaign. But that one campaign would have been a little less valuable for one of the audiences. And so we just made the decision that we were going to do two campaigns. It was, it's a, it's a lot more work. But that, that speaks to something very important about the art of email in general, and how we think about these things is that we're, we're not trying to do marketing for everybody. And we're trying to do marketing specific to the individual. So if somebody already owned autoresponder madness, the campaign that they got, you know, had a different, it had different messaging. We wanted to draw attention to the art of email, wanted to let them know how to find it, what the schedule was. And the, the audience who didn't own autoresponder madness, we needed to talk a lot more about the art of email, and less about the live workshop. So that, that's part of the reason why there are two tracks here is really this idea of speaking specifically to your audience, even if it's more work, it's the right thing to do, I think we think at least has demonstrated by our behavior. Exactly. Right. It was a lot, it was a lot more work. So we hope that was the right thing to do. Yeah. I mean, what's interesting is, is you guys are listening to this now. Some of you went on this, on this top journey, and some of you went on this bottom journey. But there's nobody that went on both journeys, for that makes sense. Those, that, that moment in which you decided you're going to bar this workshop is different for everybody. It happened at different points and it happened on different tracks. And that's kind of interesting, right? It happened in different contexts too. I mean, if you think about broadly speaking, you know, one group of one group, one context for one group was they're already familiar with with autoresponder madness. They, they had purchased the course. So now they're getting, that's a very different context than someone who isn't familiar with the course at all and purchased the course and the, and the workshop. And that's a very different context. So having, having different ways to communicate with those people specifically. It's important. And the emails are pretty different. I mean, if you look at the two series side by side, there are plenty of places where they overlap and we could reuse descriptions and schedules and feedback we receive. But the reality is they're, they're two very different campaigns. If you, if you were to look at each of them side by side, you would see where they were similar. So they got the 24th email as their very first newsletter from us. In that newsletter, it pointed back to the previous newsletter. And then there was also, we mentioned that, that we did this hand raising. And if anybody didn't get that, or they joined after that, just hit reply, let us know. And at that point, we, we, we just manually added people to the segment, which is why it's the dotted line. We didn't want to, although we could have, we didn't want to have the hard bridge inside of that, that newsletter, because that's what we said. We just wanted to be congruent with what we said. And, you know, we said that we were going to do these, these, these hand raisings as a separate things and not part of the, the news that is, but there's no reason why you can't do it as part of the news that is, and we've done that many times in the past. It's just for this campaign, the way that we decided to, to run this, for the reasons that we explained just now, we, you know, it's slightly more complicated when, when, when there's multiple segments, when it's just a single segment, it's a lot easier just to add these, these, these hand raising opportunities into a newsletter as well, wherever else. So that's why this is a dotted line over there. And if we had wanted to do that, so everyone understands the mechanisms to do that, we could have sent, we could have segmented the value newsletter for the 24th or emergent marketing newsletter, we could have sent one, one batch of those to ARM customers and given them an interest list link for that appropriate interest list, we could have done, we could have essentially mirrored what we did in the bridging emails, but we, you know, I don't know, it's funny thinking about this, like some of the decisions we make like this are more intuitive, like, you know, you wrote the, the, the September 10th into 24th were written by us as individuals, and that you can see that we say in each of them, you know, Andre wrote the first one, it's about creating better prospects, like what you do before they become a lead. And then the second part is what happens after. And you said right up front in the first email, we're doing this because the art of emails coming out and we're talking, we're talking about email in general. So there's something about that sort of, that there was that plus mentioning that we were going to send out a separate email, like those two things together, it's not, it's not cut and dry, it's not definitive, but something about the way that context existed, it would have felt a little incongruent if on the 24th we had another interest list just by the nature of it, but certainly somebody else can make a different decision. So don't, you know, nothing that we're saying here today is like, you have to do it this, well, I'm sure. Now that I've said that, I'm sure I'll contradict exactly that. But it's not, when you think about how we describe this, it's not like, okay, here we're giving you a set of rules, and you need to do it exactly this way. It's just, these are frameworks and you're, you may have different decision points that influence how you make your decisions, and we'll try to articulate how we make ours. So you understand the thinking behind it. So, okay, sorry, off to the next point. That's a good point. I mean, with these frameworks and these, this way of assembling stuff is you get to assemble your version, and it'll look very different to our version, but they will fundamentally behave the same. There will be a PLS. So once this campaign starts, so this product launch series over here, at this point, it's completely closed. It's only going to these segments. Nobody else, anybody else entering our newsletter at this point or any of our other entry points, is they don't even get to see this. It's like, it doesn't even exist in the world. Yeah, so that's, that's always going to draw attention to a third audience that's not, that's not on this visual, but that's, it's probably the most important audience in many ways, which is everybody who didn't raise their hand in our broader audience, who said, you know, who didn't say, Hey, at this time, I'm interested, whether or not they were an ARM customer or not an ARM customer, the overwhelming majority of people within our larger audience did not raise their hand. So they're not seeing any of this because they've said with their behavior that they're not interested in this right now. So because of that, we're, it's essentially, we're respecting their inattention. You know, for everyone who is participating, we're respecting their attention and say, Okay, you've raised your hand, you said you're interested, we're going to take you on this journey. Everybody else who didn't, we're saying, Okay, we're respecting your inattention, and we're not going to show you. And we don't, and when we say that, we don't, that's, we're not saying that, but then doing something differently. You know, we're not trying to like tomorrow, we'll send an emergent marketing newsletter tomorrow, and there's no like last chance to act on this thing that's been happening that you said you weren't interested in. There's none of that. Because if we, if we, if we make those lines fuzzy, if we say, Okay, we're going to, we're going to tailor our emails based on behavior and interest and intent. But then we kind of don't sometimes then, then we're kind of smearing the edges a little bit. So most people who are in our larger audience, have no idea what's happened over the last two weeks, which is, you know, in and of itself is kind of a, it's an amazing thing to think about. You don't have to be in front of everybody all the time to have a successful business. And in fact, we would argue that's exactly the wrong way to try to do things. Because if someone isn't interested, why would you, why would you want to be showing them all this? This is a lot of cognitive load for somebody. I mean, even if, for those of you who raised your hand and said, Yes, I want to, I mean, you have a week, we've captured a lot of your attention. Each email is somewhere around 1200 words every day, over 10 days. That's, that's a 20% of a novel to read over the course of two weeks. To do that when you're interested is one thing, to have that in front of you when you're not interested at that point in time. That's, that's counterproductive at best. All right, off we go with the next thing. Right. So the thing that's, that's, that's not shown here is that because this is, this has a open and a closed date. And there's, there's a moment when we start this promotion, which, which means we write this first broadcast email and send it out to the segment. Now, this could be a single segment, but in our case, we were sending it out to two different segments, hence the, hence the two lines here. But on Monday, the 27th is when we sent this first email. Now, these, these emails here still exist. They're, they're out there in people's inboxes and not everybody clicks on as much as we would love that is, you know, as soon as emails hit inboxes, people don't, don't open them and go and click the links. So there's a small percentage of people that will click these links after the fact that could click them here, here, here, here, that would click them in the middle of a promotion or in the last day of a promotion. So what we do is we, we create an easy error. You call it easy error, which is that when any of these links are clicked over here, it triggers, it triggers a sequence that then that's, that's just one email and that one email just puts people in the loop. It's, hey, notice that you've, you've, you've just come into this party late. Here's what you've missed out on so far. And then it'll list. So, so let's say on Tuesday, on Tuesday, we've sent Monday's email. So I'll just go and edit easy error. So we put two of them because there's, there's two segments here. I'll edit it and I will add Monday's email to that list. So the archive link of Monday's email to that list and just save that email. So if any person clicks that link and ends up here one day late, they've got a link to, to that email. If somebody segments themselves on Friday over here, so October 1st, that, that exact same email that they will receive will have links to the 30th, 29th, 28th, 27th. So every single person's always in the loop no matter how late they come to the party. And that's what the, that's what the easy error does, which is not shown here. So we just wanted to draw your attention to that, that even though somebody can segment it late, and it does happen, it's in this case, it was around about 5%. And so the, the easier segment is about 5% of the size of these ones here. So there are people coming in late and, you know, we, we want them to get the context as quickly as possible. And for that, for that email that they're suddenly receiving. So if they've segmented themselves on October 1st, they're suddenly going to receive emails in the middle of a promotion, which, which can be jarring if there's no mechanism for them to catch up. So that's why we do that. And we have that fail safe too for people who have been on the list, who have been getting the emails, because again, you know, it would be really nice if all of you would read each email the day we send it. But we realize a lot of people don't. So you'll notice in Monday through Friday, the first Monday through Friday, that each email that was sent included in links at the very beginning to all of the previous emails, and mentioned, you know, read these first, because we're building up to something. So it's, you know, we're, we're, we want to be aware of how everybody spends their attention. And, you know, some people like Andre just mentioned, they just come in late and we have to catch them up, but others, and maybe they didn't read Wednesday, you know, maybe Monday and Tuesday, whatever, you know, they get to the office, it's busy. And they see Wednesday's email and they jump into Wednesday's email, but they, they haven't seen Monday and Tuesday yet. We really want to get them to go back, essentially, and to start at the beginning and encourage people to do that, because this is, these are not, we didn't write 10 individual emails. This is a campaign, it's a, you can see where there's a theme and there's a narrative arc within a larger narrative arc, and to just get dropped into the middle of that. It can be jarring, maybe like just, you know, dropping into a television series and, you know, lost episode number, or, you know, 24 episode number eight. Like there's, it's very jarring, all kinds of things are happening, but you don't know how you got there. So we're, we're giving all of these opportunities for people to orient to where are they, how did they get there, what does today, before you, you know, take the next step on the journey, let's make sure you, you know where you are. So lots of, lots of what we do, I don't think we use this term ever, but we want to make sure that everybody who comes into a campaign has a map to where they are, making orient themselves to it. Yeah, exactly. So Pride was the, was the theme we decided that was going to propel our, so these emails here before the cart opened is our kind of like our pre-launch, and we used Pride as, as the theme. So if any, so if anybody suddenly came in on, on a Wednesday and we're talking about this thing called Pride, that's, that's kind of why it's important that people, that we give people ways to go back and then consume emails that we know were received. Well, you know, those, that first email, we got so many responses from it because it resonated with people. So, and again, that was, that was amazing how many people replied to that email. Yeah, I mean, Sean and Arwen, that wasn't forwards to try and figure out what is, what is the theme that we're going to use to, to kind of draft this, this, this campaign forward. And when when we had a call before this, and we were trying to discuss, you know, figure out what that was, then it finally came to us. And so that created the frame in which everything else exists now. So even though there's, there's emails further along here that don't even talk about Pride, people that are reading these emails received that, that email. So that's part of their, their, their mental framework. It's, it's, it's, it's part of that journey that they've been on, where they've received this email about Pride, they understand what that means and in the context. And it's something that, that excites them. So everything else just keeps building on to, to different things. Yeah, and something that's not obvious here is this, we, we write these emails on the day or the day before, based on, on the response that we, that we get. So, so these PLS series, these, these promotions, they, they are very organic. They just, you know, you can't plan for this whole thing. We can, we can have a plan for what we want the, what we think the theme is going to be. But outside of that, you know, the actual nuances of each, we had no idea what email on the 1st of October was going to look like over here. No, no idea. Okay, and I think that's a, that's something that, that speaks to the theme and narrative arc that I think is really important that this, it doesn't have to be complicated. This isn't like, this isn't literary, you know, in depth, you know, what's the, you know, Romeo and Juliet or David and Goliath, like, you know, if you don't have to, that's not, doesn't have to be what theme is. Theme can be as simple as, as a single word, right? Like this is, then we use pride. Like that, that became like the lens through which it's, it's the tie that connects everything. You know, we'll, and it, it's the frame that connects things. Like it's, it's just, it's the thing that brings you back home as the writer all the time. It's the, and it's the thing that the subconsciously, the audience can continue to hang on from one to the next. And we describe pride in different ways. You know, we just have our, it had many meanings. So it gave us lots of opportunity to be congruent with that message. You know, there's the pride that we feel having created the art of email and that experience. There's the pride that someone who goes through the art of email can feel as they make progress and as they get results. There's that sort of larger pride. You know, there's the internal pride somebody can feel. There's the external pride of, you know, people in your life who depend on you, who reflect back on you that pride. I mean, there are all kinds of ways pride can manifest. So by choosing that, we gave ourselves a pretty wide category to draw from every single email. And, you know, we, we know that there are certain emails that need to do certain things. You know, we have one of the terms we use, which I love is, you know, the what's in the box email. I think we even use that subject on, which I love, which, you know, we know one email before enrollment opens. We need to make sure that we don't forget to actually tell people what the thing is, right? And what's in the box. So, you know, we kind of know they're going to be there, different places. We know the first email kind of frames everything. That's an important one. We know the last email is the opportunity to just bring everything to a close to thank everyone to make sure that we've, we've been explicit about what a privilege it is to interact with an audience. When we do enrollments, we always include a free gift that goes out today, actually. So we know somewhere along the back half, we're going to have an email that's just devoted to, you know, in this case, it's a free mini course. So we have these kind of placeholders, but everything else, you know, like Andre just said, you know, there's no Machiavellian sketch storyboard of, you know, we're going to do that. And we wrote two months ago. And we, you know, we wrote today's email this morning. You know, we wrote, you know, in last week, and when you see in last week's emails, you'll see there, you know, we've included things that people have said about the heart of email, whether it's an email back to us in response to one of the enrollment emails or a comment within the heart of email. Well, those, we got those like that morning, like we, we were looking through like the feedback we received the day before that that day. So there's, it's very important, I think very important to draw attention to this idea that this is the writing of these emails is a very organic process. And it's, you know, it's intense too. I mean, it's, this is no joke. There's a lot of moving parts in this. And it's, it's, it's legit. Yeah, I mean, one of the things I'll mention it quickly, because it's not the focus of this call right here, but it's worth mentioning just to, just for context is that these PLS, as Sean just said, are very organic. We don't know what we're going to write necessarily until we've in the moment, and we're just feeding off the feedback other than certain parts over here. I think, I think it was on Wednesday, it's, as Sean said, what's in the box email. And in fact, that one had, I think so far, it's had the highest open rate, and makes sense. People want to know what's in the box, right? I like that. But what we do here in this, in this mealy of stuff, this informs later on, when we, when we turn this into a story powered promotion, we've got all the emails we've written, we've experienced this in the moment, we, it's all been based over feedback. And we get then, then to construct a story powered promotion for an evergreen version of this, that can be triggered at any point in time. But we're not writing that, that SPP, that story powered promotion called, we are using this to inform what we write then. But again, that's not the point of this call, but it's something that you'll see later on. Yeah, I think I'll, before we make sure we don't forget it, I'll draw attention to the dollar signs on the bottom there. So one understands what that means. So you'll see the dollar signs are placed under when the card opens and when the card closes. Our experience may be different than everybody else's. I think I'm guessing it is in one particular way, which I'll draw attention to in a second. But in general, what we've seen across many enrollments is we tend to get about, you know, the total sales, we tend to get about 30% of total sales, somewhere around 30% of total sales on day one. And then we tend to get somewhere 35 to 40% of sales on the last day. Everything else happens in between. And there's often what we call a trough of despair. There's often two or three days at a certain point where you just, you wonder what you've done. It's very common. But it's not, you know, with a PLS, day one, you have the people who are most motivated. And that kind of gives you a signal about the whole campaign itself. The difference I do want to draw attention to is that we are very deliberate about not amplifying sales pressure on the last day. In fact, we try to take some of the energy out of that. Conventional wisdom is all about, I hate to say wisdom in this, but conventional thinking is that on the last day is when you push, push, push. So you pile on the bonuses on the last day, increased urgency, you know, you sent, I'm sure everyone listening to this has received the six hours left, four hours left, 30 minutes left. We don't do any of that. And we actually, starting with the traffic engine, this was sort of an interesting experience with the traffic engine is that there was so much energy and excitement around it. And now we're having so much fun with that promotion and playing back and forth off each other that when we got to almost the end, I believe it was Sunday and it ended on Monday, we got to Sunday. I felt really uncomfortable all of a sudden because I thought it's, there are going to be people who've been with us this entire time who shouldn't buy, but they haven't yet, they shouldn't. But we're going to leave them feeling like they've missed out on something if they don't buy and how could we, you know, what could we do instead? So that the traffic engine was an eight-week course. So what we did for the traffic engine the first time, as we said, let's go, let's create a companion, we call it a mini marketing MBA, eight-week course, so that we could give to everyone and say, listen, if the traffic engine's not right for you right now, don't feel any pressure buy it instead. Here's this thing you can do for eight weeks. You can find that on our site. It's incredible, such a great course, tons of resources that you really, we believe are just definitive resources. So that became a thing, we do that all the time now. And we do it because there are people who haven't purchased yet, who they haven't purchased because, when I say yeah, I mean it's one day before the car closes, they haven't purchased, but they know they're going to, it's just a matter, they just haven't gotten around to it. And that's fine, we know a bunch of them, 35, 40% of the total are going to do that. But there's this other, I don't know what the percentage is, there's no way to know, but there's this other group of people who shouldn't buy, it's not right for them. And we could, if we wanted to, we could persuade some of them, we could pile some things on, we even had somebody email early on and said, I'm not sure what day to buy, will I get all of the bonuses if I buy early? It's just kind of funny to email back, they're no bonuses, we're not, we don't do that nonsense. And that was someone new in our world. But the course, the free mini course that we're sending today is designed to make sure that that whatever percentage of people who shouldn't buy, don't buy because they don't want to leave empty-handed. That's just a really important consideration. So I just wanted to draw some attention to that. Yeah, that's a good point. So, which is here. And then Friday's email is just, it's just a very, you know, it's all ending now. And it's really just drawing attention to if they're ready to buy, if they might don't buy. Yeah, so I think there's one other thing to mention here too, which we haven't talked about, you'll see on the drawing, we have their nine dots, and then you've got the two asterisks there under the fifth. So the original plan was that we were going to do a Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, and week one, nothing on the weekend. And then Monday, Wednesday, Friday, that was sort of the initial plan. And Friday, we're going to send out the free mini course. Then we, I don't know, maybe sometime last week, so, you know, there's that I was saying, no plan survives contact with reality. So sometime last week, we realized, well, let's send, we want to draw attention to the free mini course. Let's send out on Thursday. We'll send it before everything closes, and it'll be a chance to just be that. And there's no distraction. And it's just that people can go see like, what's the mini course all about. And on Monday, this week, the fourth, we sent an email. And, you know, I felt that this really, I felt like there was, there was more to be said, and we could have janded into Wednesday, but there was, when I, when I, we were thinking about writing Monday's email, it got me thinking more broadly about Monday's email, when you, if you go back and reread both of Monday's emails really are focused on questions and answers. And because of that, that had my mind was really racing around like, wow, people buy for lots of different reasons. People, a lot of, you know, kind of spectrum of emotion versus logic and, you know, outcome versus experience. And there are lots of reasons people buy. And that was so stuck in my head that I, you know, I got up on Tuesday morning and just, I had to get some ideas out of my head and then handed them off to Andre to say, look, I think, I think this is valuable for everybody to understand. It makes sense within the context of this campaign. It's important. It's, it's standalone important, and it deserves its own email. It wasn't that, that email wasn't if you can go back and read Tuesday's, and that's not designed to sell. It's not, there's no hard sell in it. It's just, it's part of this larger ethos of what are all the things people need to know to understand what we're talking about? Well, that was a category of things we felt like they needed to know, but we didn't, in advance, we didn't, that wasn't on our radar, but because of something that we did in the campaign that emerged and it deserved its own place. So now, you know, now we have 10 emails instead of nine. It's a wonder we don't have 50 emails because we get a million ideas. So that's, that's what those two asterisks mean. Which messed up my, my image here because I couldn't add it in afterwards. It's still a little asterisk. I don't have to figure out how to fix that for you. So what else, what else, what happened when we spoke in about here? Yes, I'm looking through the list here. I made a list. And this is a, when I say I'm looking through a list here, this is a perfect opportunity to mention any comments about my handwriting are completely lies. So if you see anything in Andre's name about how my handwriting looks like a doctor's, this is not true. You just can't, can't read my handwriting. It's too perfect. Yeah. I mean, that's, I think, I think we can probably switch to, you know, to wrapping it up. I mean, I, I know I love overviews. So I think, you know, like when I step back at 10,000 feet, I think, okay, what in the hell am I looking at here? Like what, what is this? Right? So at the highest level, you know, we wrote the manifesto this summer, right? We do it because we really, it was hard. It was funny creating the art of email. It was really hard to create parts of it until we created the parts before it. And we realized this summer that we had to write the manifesto to before the intro made sense because the intro needed to have a bunch of stuff in it. But it also, the intro doesn't exist by itself. The intro comes in a certain context. So I think it was June or July, maybe July that we wrote the manifesto. So the manifesto is, you know, sort of, it is at a maybe 30,000 foot view, the manifesto had formed everything here. It's really sort of the core of who we are. So when you see that top theme narrative arc, it really is this idea that modern email marketing is changing, right? Apple in particular is making a lot of changes on privacy. Email as we know it is changing. And it's time that if you want to do email marketing really well, there's need to do it differently. Like the things that most people do make sense in sort of an old view of reality. They don't make sense in a modern view of reality. So that's really the thing that everything, that's the umbrella that everything fits underneath. And then for a lot of people, their experience was they, we've had the art of email on their radar all summer. We expected, initially we thought we might do this in the spring. But that we sort of mentioned, used to be autoresponder madness for was the idea and we were going to rename it. We didn't reveal the name until September 10th. Or did we reveal it on the 14th? No, the 14th. So the September 10th, you know, some, there's some group of people read the newsletter on the 10th. They were not customers of ARM. They saw the notification on the 14th, a bridge email, then they saw the manifesto on the 21st, they raised their hand. And then they had this, they had, are continuing to have this experience that has another narrative arc within the larger narrative arc. But the, what I think the last thing I'll draw attention to today is something that's very important to both of us, which is that we don't teach things that number one, aren't what we do. And that's, it's shockingly common. I know, certainly from my experience working with names, people listening to this would recognize I was more, way more often than not stunned that what the person was teaching had no relationship whatsoever to how they behaved. So we're both sensitive to that. So you're going through and hearing how we did this campaign. It's not, there's no daylight between this and what you learn in the art of email for a variety of reasons. One, we believe this is what works best. And two, this is who we are. So we're not going to tell you to do something that we're not going to deal with, that we don't believe is true. So what you see here is our expression of those frameworks. We make some decisions. Others might not make decisions like not having an interest list link on the 24th. Others could just as easily make a different decision. It's not right. It's not wrong. It's just this reflects how we think about things. What's beautiful about the art of email is that your, it's that idea from like yesterday's email on the kaleidoscope, right? That you, you could take the same idea that we're presenting here and you could turn the kaleidoscope with the same ingredients, just a little bit differently. And you might have a different expression of the same thing that we're doing. But then it's this, it's that kaleidoscope all the way down. So it's the kaleidoscope for us and you. But then for every individual who comes through this, they're the way they turn the kaleidoscope is they see something just a little bit different. You know, someone might have read the newsletter, skipped the, the 14th, the bridge email on the 14th, read the manifesto, got excited about it, skipped two or three of the emails in this series, read one, wanted to figure out where they were, went back to the beginning. You know, others aren't going to read anything. They're going to get to tomorrow's email and go, Oh, I got to get caught up. And they're going like, it's just everything is, it's, it's not, you know, every person gets this, you know, programmatically different experience, we're not suggesting that, but there are hundreds of variations of experience, maybe thousands of variations of experience within this larger idea, which is just the expression of how we did it. And you could take these same ingredients for something that you're doing and turn them ever so slightly and have a different experience and your audience would have a different experience. And that's, that's what I love so much about the, the art of email. And this is only part of it. And this is only, you know, we have one, two, three, we don't have the relationship building series in here. We don't have onboarding. We don't have story powered promotions. I mean, there's so much missing from here that it's just, it's, it's so exciting to think about the million different ways. Somebody who's listening to this, the potential that they have ahead of them to take all of these parts and make these parts into something just absolutely amazing. That's unique to how they show up in the world. And then for each person who goes through their unique expression of value, that person can find a different perspective on it that's meaningful to them. So I will close my comments today, returning to the theme of pride, which is I've, you know, I'm 50. I've been in the game for 26 years. I've, I'm very proud of a lot of my accomplishments. But this is what, what we've done here with the art of email, I think about as a, it's, it's a top five professional accomplishment for me. It is extraordinary to see how all of this works and to see what's possible with it. So no pressure, Andre, I'll just set you up with that. And this is your, this is your brainchild.
